claim: The traceability matrix's per-REQ file lists were never reconciled to the as-built layout; they still mirror the plan's literal layout, which the implementation deviated from in two documented places. (1) REQ-001/REQ-008 list only src/main/status/orky-osc-parser.ts, but the OrkyHeartbeat public-interface artifact was relocated to src/shared/types.ts (to avoid a main->shared import cycle; the parser only re-exports it) and the matrix carries no note of that move. (2) REQ-008/REQ-009 list src/main/services.ts and src/main/ipc/register-orky.ts as 0014 touch-points, but the OrkyStreamStatusBridge is actually composed in src/main/ipc/register.ts (services.ts is untouched; register-orky.ts is unmodified, only receiving the shimmed orkySend from register.ts), and register.ts -- the real composition root -- is absent from the matrix. The shipped feature doc (docs/features/orky-osc-heartbeat.md Architecture table) and the code comments are accurate; only this internal traceability artifact lags, so a future maintainer doing impact analysis is pointed at two files that were not changed and away from the two that were.
fix: Reconcile traceability.json/.md to the as-built tree: add src/shared/types.ts (OrkyHeartbeat home) to REQ-001 and REQ-008 with a one-line deviation note that the plan placed it in orky-osc-parser.ts; in REQ-008/REQ-009 replace src/main/services.ts (untouched) and src/main/ipc/register-orky.ts (unmodified) with src/main/ipc/register.ts as the actual bridge composition root. propose CONV: the traceability matrix's per-REQ file lists MUST be reconciled to the as-built file layout (not the plan's literal layout) before the review gate, with any documented deviation noted inline.

claim: `decodeHeartbeat` enforces the 256 cap with `body.length` and then stores every `key=value` pair before validating only the known required fields, so an otherwise valid marker with an unknown pair containing non-contract bytes (for example non-ASCII, or `\x1b` not followed by `\\`) is still accepted. That violates the documented global body contract in docs/features/orky-osc-heartbeat.md:47-50 and :78-83: legal bodies are printable ASCII `[A-Za-z0-9._;=-]`, can never contain `\x07`/`\x1b`, and are capped at 256 bytes. Because the PTY stream is attacker-influenceable, this also weakens the bounded grammar: multibyte unknown values can pass a character-count cap while exceeding the byte cap.
fix: Before splitting into pairs, reject any body whose byte length exceeds 256 and reject any byte outside the contract alphabet (or at minimum reject `\x07`, `\x1b`, and non-ASCII) even when it appears in an unknown forward-compatible pair. Keep unknown keys ignored only after the body-level byte/charset invariants pass.

location: src/main/status/orky-osc-parser.ts:121-133 (OrkyOscParser.push) — root cause in src/main/status/osc-scanner.ts:18 (scanOsc, shared/pre-existing, reused per REQ-002)
claim: REQ-005 makes an explicit MUST-NOT acceptance criterion: 'The parser MUST NOT grow its buffer without bound on a stream that contains the prefix but never a terminator (it relies on scanOsc's carry-over, which retains only the pending partial sequence).' That premise is false. scanOsc's no-terminator-found branch is `if (end === -1) return buf.slice(start)` — it returns EVERYTHING from the first prefix occurrence to the end of the accumulated buffer, not a small bounded carry-over. Reproduced directly against the real algorithm (copied byte-for-byte): pushing the 12-byte ORKY_OSC prefix once, then ~1,000,000 bytes of plain 'A' characters in 100 chunks with no BEL/ST anywhere, leaves the internal carry-over buffer at 1,000,012 chars — it grows 1:1 with attacker-supplied input, unbounded. Because StatusEngine holds one OrkyOscParser per pane for the pane's entire lifetime and feeds it every raw PTY data chunk (status-engine.ts:62-65), and the spec's own threat model names exactly this attacker (a remote shell, a hostile program, or `cat`-ing a crafted file writing to the PTY), a single 12-byte prefix followed by sustained non-terminating output (trivial to produce deliberately, e.g. `printf '\e]8888;orky='; cat /dev/zero`) causes unbounded heap growth in the privileged Electron main process for as long as the attacker keeps streaming — a memory-exhaustion DoS that can crash the whole app (all panes, all unsaved state) for one pane's worth of malicious/compromised output. The frozen test TEST-021 ('1 MiB of the bare prefix with no terminator does not throw and emits nothing') does NOT catch this: it only asserts no-throw and empty-emission, never the actual buffer/memory size, so it passes green over the unbounded-growth defect. Note the per-marker 256-byte/32-pair cap in decodeHeartbeat (REQ-004) is correctly enforced AFTER a terminator is found — but the bug is upstream of that: an attacker who never sends a terminator never reaches decodeHeartbeat at all, so that cap provides no protection here.
fix: Add an explicit size guard in OrkyOscParser.push (or scanOsc) for the no-terminator carry-over case: after scanOsc returns, if the retained buffer exceeds a small fixed ceiling (e.g. ORKY_OSC.length + MAX_BODY_BYTES + a few terminator-slack bytes — no legitimate unterminated partial marker can ever legitimately exceed that), drop/reset the buffer to '' so a never-terminated prefix cannot accumulate unbounded memory. Add a test that asserts the parser's *internal buffer size* (not just throw/emit behavior) stays bounded after pushing many MiB of prefix+non-terminating data across many chunks, closing the TEST-021 gap. Consider flagging the same unboundedness in the shared scanOsc (osc-scanner.ts) for Osc133Parser/CwdParser as a separate follow-up, since they inherit the identical no-terminator-found behavior; a scoped fix local to OrkyOscParser is sufficient to close this feature's REQ-005 violation without touching baseline-confirmed shared code.

claim: The stateful fs/stream combiner OrkyStreamStatusBridge -- the ONLY event-arrival-order-sensitive component in this feature and the actual production applier of REQ-009's filesystem-wins precedence -- has no behavioral test. Only the pure selectOrkyPaneStatus precedence rule is unit-tested (TEST-040..042); the bridge that holds per-pane Map state, applies precedence under interleaved fs/stream arrival, dedups via JSON.stringify, and clears on pane teardown is exercised by nothing (its sole test reference is the no-write-API structural check TEST-031). The current code is order-convergent and correct (each recompute is a pure function of the current slot, so the converged output is identical regardless of whether fs or stream arrived first), but a determinism regression that makes the combine arrival-order-dependent -- e.g. swapping selectOrkyPaneStatus(slot.fs, slot.stream) arguments, dropping the lastKey dedup, or unioning fs+stream features instead of strict precedence -- would pass the entire suite undetected. The spec Definition of Done explicitly expects this wiring exercised by feeding synthetic bytes.
fix: Add a behavioral unit test for OrkyStreamStatusBridge asserting order-independent convergence: drive setFsStatus-then-setStreamHeartbeat and setStreamHeartbeat-then-setFsStatus for the same pane id and assert the final emitted status is identical (filesystem wins) regardless of arrival order; assert an unchanged recompute is deduped (no duplicate emit); assert clearPane drops the pane so a later setStreamHeartbeat starts clean. propose CONV: any stateful event/source combiner whose output must be independent of event arrival order MUST have a test that feeds its inputs in more than one arrival order and asserts an identical converged emit.

claim: `decodeHeartbeat` builds `fields` by iterating every `;`-delimited pair and unconditionally overwriting `fields[key] = value` on each iteration, so a body containing the same key twice (e.g. `v=1;g=5;...;g=99`) silently keeps only the LAST occurrence with no rejection and no warning -- the validation that follows only ever sees the last-written value. The body grammar table in 02-spec.md (and the mirrored table in docs/features/orky-osc-heartbeat.md) describes the body as 'a compact, order-independent list of key=value pairs' but never states whether a key may legally repeat, and REQ-004's enumerated violations (length/pair-count caps, missing required key, out-of-range values, non-enum values) do not mention duplicate keys at all -- so 'last occurrence wins' is a reasonable implementer default, not a bug against the letter of the spec. It is, however, an untested edge case: no TEST-ID in tests/main/orky-osc-parser.test.ts exercises a repeated key, so the chosen behavior is undocumented in code and unverified by the suite -- the same general family of gap as CONV-003 ('no silent capping/truncation of a valid value'), just for key collision rather than a numeric range.
fix: Pick one of two cheap fixes and pin it with a test: (a) reject the whole marker if any key appears more than once (`if (key in fields) return null` in the field-collection loop) -- the strictest reading, consistent with this same function's existing reject-whole-on-any-ambiguity posture; or (b) keep last-occurrence-wins but add a one-line comment next to the `fields[pair.slice(0, eq)] = pair.slice(eq + 1)` line stating that is the intentional, spec-silent behavior, plus a test asserting it (`v=1;...;g=3;g=7` decodes with `gateN===7`). Either is a small, low-risk change; (a) is preferred for consistency with the rest of the function.

claim: The spec's security stance considers only MALFORMED input (strict bounded grammar, no eval, reject-whole on garbage -- the subject of FINDING-CODEX-001/SEC-001). It never considers that a WELL-FORMED marker is unauthenticated. Any process that can write to the PTY -- the remote shell, a hostile `cat crafted-file`, a compromised or merely buggy program, NOT just the Orky CLI -- can emit a perfectly valid `\x1b]8888;orky=v=1;f=<any-slug>;k=needs-input;ph=human-review;g=7;m=8;o=0;h=1;x=0;r=human-review\x07` and fabricate a misleading Orky status for ANY feature: a feature DIFFERENT from the one actually running, a fake 'needs you / awaiting human-review' that draws operator attention to a non-event, or a fake `done`/`idle` that masks the real state. Nothing binds the marker to the actual driving process, and the parser/mapper map the validated-but-unauthenticated fields straight onto the same chip 0004 renders for verified filesystem status. Since the chip drives operator attention, this is a status-spoofing / UI-integrity vector the threat model ignores -- a missing requirement that no test exercises, distinct from the malformed-input findings. Impact is bounded -- read-only presentation, no code-exec/persistence (REQ-013) -- hence MEDIUM, but it is the central unexamined assumption of the whole feature: that stream bytes carrying an Orky heartbeat over a bare-SSH pane are trustworthy enough to surface as status.
fix: State the trust boundary explicitly in docs/features/orky-osc-heartbeat.md (stream-derived status is only as trustworthy as whatever writes to the PTY -- spoofable by any program on the remote, for any feature), and visually distinguish stream-derived (untrusted) status from filesystem-derived (authoritative) status so a spoofed bare-SSH heartbeat is not presented identically to a verified local one. Where derivable, constrain a stream heartbeat's feature slug to the run/cwd the pane is actually associated with. propose CONV: any status surfaced from attacker-influenceable terminal bytes MUST be documented as untrusted and SHOULD be visually distinguished from filesystem/authoritative-sourced status.

claim: Complementary to FINDING-DET-001 (which deems the bridge order-convergent-and-correct and only flags the missing test): this is a SEMANTIC precedence gap, not a convergence/test gap. REQ-009 promises 'filesystem source wins whenever PRESENT; the stream source applies ONLY as a fallback for panes with no filesystem .orky/ source.' selectOrkyPaneStatus enforces that only spatially (a null check) and is the only thing tested. But slot.fs is null until OrkyTracker's first ASYNC reread completes, and OrkyTracker explicitly emits null on unwatch and when findOrkyRoot returns null (orky-tracker.ts:70,98). So for a LOCAL pane that DOES have a .orky/ tree, a heartbeat decoded from the stream during the fs-null window (startup before the first reread, or after a mid-life unwatch) makes the bridge EMIT a stream-derived, lower-trust status -- an observable render on a pane 0004 is supposed to own. Even the order-independence test FINDING-DET-001 proposes would not catch it: that test asserts the FINAL converged emit, whereas this is about the intermediate stream-win emit being wrong in the first place. Latent in production today only because no emitter exists (the stream slot is always null), but it activates exactly when the planned out-of-scope Orky-side emission lands and a local Orky pane streams a marker -- i.e. the precedence guarantee holds spatially but not temporally.
fix: Make the bridge suppress stream-derived status for any pane known to be filesystem-watched, rather than relying on fs being momentarily non-null: track per-pane whether an orky:watch is active (or whether fs has EVER been non-null for the pane) and prefer the last-known fs ownership through transient fs-null emits; only let the stream source surface for panes that were never fs-watched (the genuine bare-SSH case). Add a wiring-level test that drives stream-first-then-fs and fs-then-fsNull-then-stream and asserts the pane never renders stream-derived status once it is fs-watched.

location: src/main/status/orky-osc-parser.ts:66 (v !== '1' reject) / REQ-004(b) / REQ-012 drift caveat
claim: `decodeHeartbeat` rejects the WHOLE marker when `v !== '1'`, which is inconsistent with the marker's own unknown-key forward-compat philosophy (unknown keys are read-and-discarded, not rejected). Because the contract is a cross-repo agreement whose drift 'no Termhalla test can catch' (REQ-012), an ADDITIVE bump to v=2 on the separate Orky side (e.g. adding one optional field while keeping every v=1 field) would make Termhalla -- until separately updated -- silently REJECT every heartbeat and show NO stream status at all for every feature: a silent total blackout, the sharpest instance of the doc-drift failure the spec itself warns about. The REQ-012 drift caveat names m (gateM) as the drift example but never surfaces this sharper v-bump-blanks-everything failure mode, and no test or doc records the consequence of a version bump.
fix: Either treat v as a minimum-compatible version (accept v>=1 and rely on unknown-key ignore for additive fields) so an additive bump degrades gracefully instead of blanking, OR document explicitly in 02-spec.md + docs/features/orky-osc-heartbeat.md (alongside the m drift note) that any v bump is a hard breaking change requiring a coordinated Termhalla release before the Orky side ships it. propose CONV: a cross-repo wire-format version field's unknown-version handling MUST be documented with its failure mode (graceful-degrade vs hard-reject/blackout), so an additive bump on one side cannot silently blank the other.
